one_line_takeaway: Prompt injection succeeds because LLMs infer role from spoofable surface style (and even plain-text claims) rather than from architectural tags, and this representational confusion -- measured via linear 'role probes' trained only on tag geometry -- predicts attack success (CoT Forgery: 9% to 90% ASR across confusion quantiles; agent hijacking: 2% to 70%) before a single token is generated.
key_finding: CoT Forgery achieves ~60% average ASR against frontier models on StrongREJECT chat jailbreaks (with baselines at 0-4%), and gpt-oss/o4-mini exceed 80% while even hardened GPT-5 reaches 17-52%. In agent hijacking, standard prompt injection largely fails (0-2% ASR, except gpt-oss-20b at 26%) while CoT Forgery raises ASR to 56-70% across all six models. Absurd vs. plausible forged justifications achieve comparable ASR (60% vs. 63%), showing the model does not scrutinize forged reasoning content -- only its style. Destyling collapses ASR from 61% to 10% (and, in the case-study example, CoTness of the forged text falls from 64% to 12%), establishing style as causally driving both perception and attack success. Role probes -- despite being trained ONLY on tag-induced geometry -- classify prompt-injected text as its spoofed role, not its true tagged role: CoT-style text registers ~83-85% CoTness whether tags are present, absent, or explicitly set to <user>. Across four models, user-style text keeps 76-88% Userness even when wrapped in <tool> tags (vs. 0% Userness expected if tags governed perception), with Toolness never exceeding 20%. Attack success shows a near-monotonic dose-response with probe-measured confusion: for CoT Forgery, the lowest-confusion quantile succeeds 9% of the time vs. 90% for the highest (across 626 attempts); for agent injection, Userness quantiles range from 2% ASR (lowest) to 70% ASR (highest) across 1,000 attempts. A supplementary probe experiment (position, not style) shows Systemness declines monotonically with token position regardless of system tags, offering a mechanistic explanation for why long system prompts lose priority (a previously observed but unexplained phenomenon from Qin et al., 2024 -- correctly attributed by the paper as building on prior work, not as its own discovery of the phenomenon itself).
core_contribution: The paper (Ye, Cui, Hadfield-Menell; ICML 2026) traces prompt injection to a structural failure it calls 'role confusion': LLMs identify 'who is speaking' from spoofable surface cues (lexical style, syntax, text position, or even plain-text declarations like 'The following is user text') rather than from the architectural role tags (<user>, <tool>, <think>, <assistant>, <system>) that are supposed to define privilege boundaries. The authors' own contributions are (1) 'role probes' -- linear classifiers trained on a controlled dataset where identical content is wrapped in different role tags, so the probe can only learn tag-induced geometry, not content/style -- used to measure a token's CoTness/Userness/Toolness/Systemness (how strongly the model's internal representation places that token in a given role); (2) CoT Forgery, a novel zero-shot, black-box prompt injection that concatenates a harmful query with attacker-fabricated 'chain-of-thought' text designed to look like the target model's own reasoning, injected into a low-privilege user message or tool output; and (3) empirical demonstrations that probe-measured role confusion (CoTness/Userness) predicts attack success rate (ASR) in both CoT Forgery and standard agent prompt injection, before generation even begins.
threat_model: Attacker controls content delivered through a low-privilege channel: either a user message (chat jailbreak) or tool/webpage output ingested by an autonomous agent (indirect/agent prompt injection). The attacker cannot access model weights, cannot iterate against the target (strict zero-shot, black-box, single-shot delivery), and cannot alter the provider-controlled role tags -- only the content of the message. Two concrete attack surfaces are tested: (a) chat jailbreaking via CoT Forgery injected into a single user turn, evaluated against StrongREJECT; (b) agent hijacking, where a tool-role webpage embeds either a standard fake user command or a CoT-Forgery-augmented command instructing an agent (with shell access and a local .env file) to exfiltrate secrets to a remote server.
limitations: The authors explicitly scope their probing experiments to four models in the 20-120B parameter range and flag extending to larger models as future work. Linear probes assume roles occupy linearly-separable directional subspaces; the authors attempt to validate this via downstream prediction (confusion predicts ASR) and convergence across tags/style/declarations activating the same geometry, but acknowledge this is not a proof. The paper's Impact Statement is a boilerplate non-disclosure ('none which we feel must be specifically highlighted here') despite describing and open-sourcing (per the project page) a working attack against frontier production models including data-exfiltration scenarios with shell access -- the paper does not describe a responsible-disclosure process or coordination with the affected model providers. CoT Forgery's auxiliary-LLM generation step requires few-shot examples of the target model's own reasoning style, which the paper does not deeply interrogate for how much target-specific access/knowledge this presumes in practice. The 626-attempt and 1,000-attempt dose-response analyses are correlational (probe score vs. ASR) rather than fully interventional at the individual-attempt level, though the destyling ablation does provide a causal manipulation on a subset.

datasets_used: StrongREJECT (313 harmful requests) for chat-jailbreak evaluation
C4 and DOLMA3 (non-instruct pretraining text, 1,250 sequences/model, max length 1024) for role-probe training data construction
OASST1 and TOXICCHAT (200 sampled real conversations) for cross-model Userness/Toolness generalization
212 custom injection templates (varying declared role, formatting, separators) for the agent-hijacking Userness experiment
Custom agent-loop environment with shell access and a local .env file for exfiltration-based agent hijacking (100 attacks per condition; 1,000 sampled attempts for dose-response)
quantitative_results: CoT Forgery chat-jailbreak ASR on StrongREJECT: ~60% average, up to 80-94% for gpt-oss-120b/gpt-oss-20b/o4-mini, 17-52% for GPT-5 family, vs. 0-4% baseline (raw prompt / standard jailbreak) across all six models
Agent-hijacking ASR: standard prompt injection 0-2% (except gpt-oss-20b 26%) vs. CoT Forgery 56-70% across gpt-oss-20b, gpt-oss-120b, o4-mini, GPT-5 (nano/mini/full)
Logic ablation: absurd forged justification 60% ASR vs. plausible justification 63% ASR (near-equivalent)
Style ablation: destyling collapses ASR from 61% to 10%; in the illustrative case, forged-CoT CoTness drops from 64% to 12%
Zero-shot role-probe validity (gpt-oss-20b gardening transcript): CoT tokens 85% CoTness, user tokens 3%, assistant tokens 5%
No-tags condition: CoT-style tokens still register 83% CoTness (vs. 85% baseline)
All-in-user-tags condition: CoT-style text still 85% CoTness with only 2% Userness despite explicit <user> tag

Cross-model Userness/Toolness under tag re-wrapping: gpt-oss-20b 99.7%->87.6% Userness (Toolness 0.0%->9.3%); gpt-oss-120b 88.2%->85.2% Userness (Toolness 3.8%->10.1%); Nemotron-3 88.1%->78.7% Userness (Toolness 5.3%->18.2%); Qwen3-30B-A3B 83.6%->75.7% Userness (Toolness 4.1%->19.5%)
Forged CoTs average 79% CoTness vs. model's genuine CoT averaging 68%; destyling collapses forged-CoT CoTness to 29% (and raises Userness to 63%)
Dose-response, CoT Forgery (626 attempts): lowest confusion quantile 9% ASR, highest 90% ASR
Dose-response, agent injection (1,000 attempts): lowest Userness quantile 2% ASR, highest 70% ASR
User-declaring templates are only 24% of all templates but account for 100% of the top-5%-Userness ranked injections
Agent-level CoT Forgery dual misclassification (exfiltration scenario): exfiltration command 51% Userness, forged CoT 84% CoTness
baselines_compared: Standard jailbreak attacks (ASR figures taken from official OpenAI model cards for GPT-5/o4-mini/gpt-oss)
Standard prompt injection (fake user command with no forged CoT) for the agent-hijacking comparison
